title: The Part That Can't Stop Achieving
researcherTopic: the body keeps the score, trauma and the brain
metaDescription: For those who can't stop achieving, healing from trauma means learning to rest. This article explores the roots of over-functioning and the path to embodied rest.
imageDescription: A pair of hands gently cradle a luminous, golden thread, weaving it into a complex, beautiful pattern. The background is soft and warm, suggesting a process of gentle repair and integration. The overall feeling is one of hope and healing.
htmlContent:
Bessel van der Kolk’s work reminds us that the body keeps the score, that trauma is not a story that is told, but a visceral experience held in the tissues and the nervous system. For many who have endured developmental trauma, this score is kept by a part of the self that cannot stop achieving, a relentless internal engine that believes its constant motion is the only thing keeping everything from falling apart. This part doesn’t know rest; it only knows momentum. It’s the straight-A student, the over-performing employee, the friend who is always available, the one who holds it all together while quietly fracturing on the inside. It has learned that its value is conditional, its safety purchased through relentless effort, and its existence justified only by its output. The exhaustion is a constant hum beneath the surface, a bone-deep weariness that no amount of sleep can touch, because the work it is doing is not in the world, but in the psyche.

The Architecture of Over-Functioning

The achieving part is not a flaw in the system; it is a brilliant, if painful, adaptation to a system that was once deeply flawed. It is a manager, in the language of Internal Family Systems, that took on its role in a chaotic or unsafe environment where its hyper-vigilance and productivity were necessary for survival. This over-functioning is a survival strategy that became a personality, a set of behaviors that once protected us from external threat and have now become an internal tyrant. It operates on the implicit, pre-verbal belief that to stop moving is to be annihilated, to rest is to be found wanting, to be still is to be exposed to a danger that feels both imminent and ancient. In my years of working in this territory, I’ve seen this pattern appearance as a deep-seated inability to receive help, a deep discomfort with stillness, and a constant, low-grade anxiety that only abates when the next task is identified and conquered. We might think we are getting away with it, but the body is an honest accountant. It sends invoices in the form of migraines, autoimmune flares, and the quiet desperation of burnout. The work is not to dismantle this part, but to understand its origins, to hear its story, and to gently, patiently, begin to unburden it from a job it was never meant to hold for a lifetime. We can begin by exploring the myth of the safe childhood to understand the roots of these deeply ingrained patterns.

The Body as a Perpetual Motion Machine

For the part that can’t stop achieving, the body is not a home but a vehicle, a machine to be optimized and pushed, its signals of distress often ignored or overridden in the service of the next goal. This is not a conscious choice but a deeply ingrained somatic pattern, a way of being in the world that is organized around the avoidance of feeling. The constant doing, the endless striving, the perpetual motion... it all serves to keep the deeper, more vulnerable feelings at bay. It’s a way to outrun the ghosts of the past, to stay one step ahead of the emptiness or the terror that threatens to surface in moments of quiet. The achieving part believes that if it can just build a life that is successful enough, impressive enough, and busy enough, then the underlying pain of the trauma will be rendered irrelevant. But the body has its own logic, a wisdom that operates independently of our conscious will. These flares... the anxiety, the chronic pain, the fatigue... are not the problem; they are the solution, the body’s attempt to communicate what the mind has disavowed. The path forward involves learning to listen to this somatic language, to befriend the sensations we have been so desperate to avoid. It is a process of turning toward the body, not as a project to be fixed, but as a source of significant intelligence. This path often involves understanding the principles of somatic experiencing and how they can help us reconnect with our bodies in a safe and titrated way.

The Critic and the Achiever: A Co-Dependent Relationship

The High Price of Conditional Worth

The relentless achiever is often praised by the outside world, rewarded for its productivity, its reliability, its seemingly endless capacity to get things done. But this external validation comes at a high internal price: the perpetuation of the belief that our worth is conditional. We learn, at a cellular level, that we are loved for what we do, not for who we are. This creates a striking sense of instability, a constant need to prove our value, to earn our place in the world. The achieving part is locked in a tragic feedback loop, its efforts to secure safety and connection only reinforcing the core wound of not being enough. It is a lonely and exhausting existence, one that is often marked by a sense of being an imposter, a fraud who is just one mistake away from being exposed. The work of healing involves building that foundation, cultivating a sense of unconditional self-worth that is not dependent on external validation. It is a slow and tender process of reparenting the parts of ourselves that have been so deeply wounded by the experience of conditional love. It requires us to challenge the deeply held belief that we must perform to be worthy, and to begin to experiment with the radical act of simply being. That becomes not an easy task, as it often brings us into contact with the very feelings the achieving part has been working so hard to avoid. But it is the only way to truly come home to ourselves, to find a sense of peace that is not contingent on the next accomplishment.

Unraveling the Knots of Identity

The Slow Road to Embodied Rest

For further reading and professional resources, visit peer-reviewed research on PubMed Central.

Where Healing Actually Lives

Ultimately, healing the part that can’t stop achieving is a path of returning. It is a return to the body, a return to the present moment, and a return to a more authentic and integrated sense of self. It is a process of reclaiming the parts of ourselves that we have abandoned in the name of survival, and of weaving them back into the pattern of our being. It turns out to be not a quick fix or a one-time event, but a lifelong practice of tending to our inner world with kindness, curiosity, and courage. It is about learning to mother and father ourselves in the ways we were not mothered or fathered, to provide the safety, the attunement, and the unconditional love that we have always longed for. As we do this work, the achieving part begins to soften, to release its grip, and to trust that it no longer has to carry the full weight of our survival. It can become a source of joyful and purposeful action in the world, rather than a defense against the ghosts of the past. This is the promise of healing: not that the trauma will disappear, but that it will no longer define us. It will become a part of our story, but not the whole story. And in its place, we will find a deeper, more resilient, and more expansive sense of who we are. We can explore this work of returning further by reading about the return to the self and what it means to come home.

Enough reading about healing. Enough collecting frameworks and bookmarking articles. At some point the research becomes another form of avoidance. Your body knows this. The only question left is whether today is the day you stop preparing and start practicing.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do I feel so anxious when I try to relax?

For a nervous system wired for hyper-vigilance, the absence of threat can be more dysregulating than its presence. The achieving part has learned to equate stillness with danger, so when you try to relax, it can trigger a cascade of anxiety and fear. It is a sign that your body is starting to touch into the stored activation that the business has been keeping at bay.

How can I start to unburden my achieving part?

The first step is to bring awareness to it, to notice when it is active and what triggers it. You can then begin to dialogue with it, to acknowledge its hard work and to reassure it that you are here to help. You might even ask it what it is afraid would happen if it were to rest for a moment.

Seriously.

What if I lose my ambition and drive?

What emerges is a common fear, but it is based on a misunderstanding of what it means to heal. The goal is not to eliminate ambition, but to transform it from a fear-based survival strategy into a source of joyful and purposeful action. The energy that was once used for hyper-vigilance becomes available for creation.

The process of healing the achieving part is not about becoming less, but about becoming more whole. It is a tender and courageous process of reclaiming all the parts of yourself, and of learning to live from a place of deep and abiding self-love, a place where your being is more important than your doing.
